planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Fireworks Gomphrena is genuinely heat-loving — it grows slowly in cool spring conditions and then rapidly accelerates in summer heat. In zone 9 this annual thrives in the same conditions (intense heat, high humidity) that defeat many ornamentals, blooming prolifically from planting until frost. The distinctive starburst flower structure with long protruding pink bracts and yellow tips makes Fireworks instantly recognizable among gomphrena. Plants are self-cleaning and do not require deadheading to bloom continuously.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil — gomphrena thrives in poor soil and blooms less when over-fertilized; Fireworks is an All-America Selections award-winning annual from an unusual wild species (Gomphrena pulchella) rather than the standard globe amaranth (G. globosa). Unlike the familiar round, button-shaped flowers of common gomphrena, Fireworks produces an entirely different structure: a long, cylindrical head with protruding hot-pink bracts tipped with yellow, creating an effect that genuinely resembles a small exploding firework from a distance — one of the most distinctive and unusual flower forms among heat-tolerant annuals. The tall, branching plant (2–3 feet) produces dozens of the unusual starburst flowers continuously through the entire summer and fall season without deadheading, making it both highly ornamental and nearly zero-maintenance once established. As one of the best annual flowers for zone 9's summer conditions — extreme heat, humidity, and occasional drought — Fireworks fills a critical role in the garden when most cool-season annuals have long since given up.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gomphrena for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Annual Flowers for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. SunPatiens are a Sakata Seed trademark and are sold as vegetatively propagated transplants only — not as seed. The Compact series plants are smaller (18–24 inches) than the Vigorous series (24–36 inches), making Compact Pink better suited to containers and formal beds. SunPatiens bloom continuously without deadheading and are self-cleaning. In zone 9 they perform well from spring through fall in both sun and partial shade, withstanding summer heat and humidity significantly better than traditional bedding impatiens.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil — SunPatiens tolerate more sun than traditional impatiens and are suitable for both shaded and full-sun locations; SunPatiens Compact Pink is part of the SunPatiens line from Sakata Seed — an interspecific impatiens hybrid (Impatiens x hybrida) developed to solve the critical problem of impatiens downy mildew (Plasmopara obducens) that devastated traditional bedding impatiens (Impatiens walleriana) populations beginning around 2011–2012. The interspecific hybrid parentage gives SunPatiens resistance to this downy mildew disease that rendered conventional impatiens unreliable for shade garden use in warm, humid climates. SunPatiens Compact Pink produces large (1.5–2 inch), bright candy-pink flowers continuously from planting until frost, with glossy, slightly succulent foliage that is more heat-tolerant than standard impatiens. The unique sun-and-shade tolerance allows SunPatiens to be used across a wider range of light conditions than any traditional impatiens variety — from deep shade to full sun — making it one of the most versatile summer annuals for varied garden situations.
sources: Sakata Seed Corporation — SunPatiens Product Line
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Impatiens Downy Mildew and Alternatives
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Annuals for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Little Ruby is grown for its foliage, not its flowers — the tiny white flowers are insignificant. In zone 10+ it persists as a perennial; in zone 9 it grows as a long-season annual that may overwinter in sheltered locations. The compact, mounding habit (12–18 inches) makes it ideal for borders, containers, and low-edging applications where the deep purple-red foliage provides consistent color contrast throughout the season. Pinching tip growth promotes dense, compact branching. In partial shade the foliage develops a deep burgundy-purple; in full sun it may become more red-toned.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Little Ruby is a compact cultivar of Alternanthera dentata (Brazilian joyweed) selected for its intensely deep purple-red foliage and compact, tidy mounding habit — an improvement over older, taller alternanthera varieties that required frequent cutting back to maintain appearance. The deep, saturated burgundy-red to purple-red leaf color provides a rich, jewel-toned contrast to chartreuse, lime-green, and silver foliage companion plants in container combinations and formal bedding designs. Alternanthera is grown entirely as a foliage plant for its color effect — the small, white, inconsequential flowers do not add to the ornamental value and the plant is typically sheared off when flowering begins to redirect energy into producing vibrant new foliage. In zone 9's hot, humid summer conditions, alternanthera thrives — heat intensifies rather than fades the foliage color, making it one of the few plants that actually looks better during zone 9's harshest summer months. Little Ruby's partial shade tolerance allows use in garden positions that receive less than 6 hours of direct sun.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alternanthera for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Foliage Annuals for Gulf Coast
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Foliage Plants

planting.note: Direct sow 4–8 weeks before first fall frost for spring bloom; in zone 9 sow October–November for February–April flowering. Victoria Blue forget-me-not is typically grown as a winter annual in zone 9 — seeds sown in fall germinate through cool weather, overwinter as small rosettes, and produce flowers in late winter through spring before setting seed and dying as temperatures rise. The self-seeding characteristic means a planting once established will typically reseed itself year after year in favorable locations. In zone 9 partial shade extends the blooming season by keeping the soil cooler and delaying bolting.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — forget-me-nots prefer cool, moist conditions and partial shade in warm climates; Victoria Blue is a compact, early-flowering forget-me-not cultivar producing an exceptional azure-blue that is one of the most intensely saturated blues available among spring-blooming cool-season plants. The tiny (0.25-inch), five-petaled flowers in the classic azure-blue with a white or yellow eye completely cover the low, mounding plant in a carpet of pure blue in late winter and spring — creating a luminous blue ground-level effect that is unmatched by any other cool-season annual. The 'Victoria' in the name refers to the compact, mounding habit selected by breeders for more formal garden use — unlike the sprawling, loose habit of wild forget-me-not, Victoria Blue forms tidy compact mounds ideal for edging, underplanting spring bulbs, and filling in between larger plants in spring garden combinations. The traditional spring combination of azure forget-me-nots with orange or yellow tulips creates one of the most visually striking color contrasts in the spring garden. In zone 9 forget-me-nots bloom in late winter (February–March), providing garden interest in the color-limited cool season.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Myosotis sylvatica Victoria Series
North Carolina State University Extension — Cool-Season Annual Flowers
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Annual Flowers for Florida

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Double Scoop Cranberry blooms in its first year from well-established container plants and becomes more productive in subsequent years. In zone 9 coneflowers perform best in well-drained to lean soils — the combination of heat and consistently moist soil causes root rot; excellent drainage and lean soil are more important in warm climates than in the North. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering, or leave some seed heads for goldfinches in fall.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil — coneflowers are native prairie plants that thrive in poor, dry soil and fail in rich, consistently moist conditions; Double Scoop Cranberry is a double-flowered coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) from the Double Scoop series, producing fully double, pompom-like flower heads in deep cranberry-red — a significant departure from the familiar single daisy form of standard purple coneflower. The double flower heads are completely filled with petals, with no central cone visible, creating a lush, dense flower of a different character than single coneflowers — more like a small dahlia than the familiar Echinacea form. The rich cranberry-red color combined with the compact (18–24 inch) habit and double flower structure makes Double Scoop Cranberry one of the most ornamentally distinctive of the new-generation Echinacea hybrids. As a native North American prairie perennial, coneflowers are strongly adapted to heat, drought, and poor soil — genuine tough plants that perform excellently once established in zone 9 with good drainage. The flowers attract butterflies and bees, and the seed heads feed goldfinches and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter.
sources: Ball Horticultural Company — Echinacea Double Scoop Series
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Echinacea purpurea Native Perennial
University of Georgia Extension — Coneflowers for Southern Gardens
